model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The failed step suggests the cosine decay was too aggressive for a 256-step budget. Reverting to a constant learning rate and doubling Adam's step size is a simple fast-convergence move that better matches the short fixed-step regime.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"With only two pooled conv blocks, the current model is very shallow for CIFAR-10. Increasing depth to three blocks adds one more nonlinear stage and reaches a stronger 12->24->48 channel hierarchy while still keeping the classifier compact, making this a high-upside one-line capacity change.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The model is optimizing under a very short budget and validation loss is the target, so a small amount of label smoothing is a cheap way to reduce overconfidence and often improves held-out cross-entropy even when accuracy changes little.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The strongest gain so far came from increasing Adam's learning rate under the fixed 256-step budget, which suggests the run is still somewhat optimization-limited. A further step up to 1.5e-3 is a simple continuation of that trend and is still a conservative range for Adam with batchnorm on CIFAR-10.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The current recipe is already pushing a fairly aggressive Adam learning rate, and decoupled weight decay often behaves better in that regime than classic Adam with L2-style decay. Switching to AdamW is a minimal optimizer-family change with a reasonable chance of improving validation loss without disturbing the rest of the setup.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The AdamW switch moved validation loss in the wrong direction, which suggests decoupled weight decay is not helping this short-budget regime. Reverting to plain Adam preserves the stronger settings found so far while removing the last unsuccessful optimizer-family change.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"With label smoothing already providing some regularization, the current weight decay may be slightly too strong for a short 256-step run. Halving weight decay is a simple way to ease underfitting while keeping the rest of the strongest recipe unchanged.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.05)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"After reducing weight decay, the current setup may now be slightly over-regularized. Keeping label smoothing but dialing it back from 0.1 to 0.05 is a targeted way to preserve the calibration benefit while allowing a bit more fit within the short training budget.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The recent gains have consistently come from easing regularization. Pushing weight decay down one more notch is a direct continuation of that trend and may free a bit more fit in the short 256-step regime without changing the overall training dynamics.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.7e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-5","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The last step indicates 2e-5 weight decay was too loose, so this restores the better 5e-5 setting and then continues the strongest trend so far: slightly higher Adam learning rates under the short fixed-step budget. A modest bump to 1.7e-3 is a targeted next probe from the current best recipe.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.7e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The best gains so far have come from progressively increasing Adam's learning rate while keeping the rest of the recipe stable. Moving from 1.7e-3 to 2e-3 is a small, clean continuation of that trend and is still a plausible range for this short-budget batchnorm model.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.7e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The jump to 2e-3 clearly overshot, so this restores the stronger 1.7e-3 setting and uses the step to test a modest width increase. Moving the three-block CNN from 12 base channels to 16 is a clean capacity change that could improve representation quality without radically altering the training recipe.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The width increase clearly hurt, so this restores the stronger base architecture and uses the next probe on data throughput instead. With a fixed step budget, doubling batch size lets the run cover more training examples per update sequence while keeping the optimizer settings intact.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The largest improvement so far came from increasing batch size under the fixed-step budget, which strongly suggests example coverage matters more than extra parameter updates here. Pushing from 128 to 256 is the cleanest next probe along that winning direction.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.9e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.7e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The batch-size increase to 256 changed the optimization regime substantially and produced the biggest gain so far, which usually creates headroom for a somewhat larger learning rate. A modest bump from 1.7e-3 to 1.9e-3 is a controlled test of that batch-size/learning-rate scaling effect without adding complexity.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.9e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The last learning-rate increase still improved validation loss at batch size 256, so there appears to be remaining headroom in the larger-batch regime. A small step to 2.1e-3 is the most direct continuation of the strongest trend without adding extra moving parts.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.9e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The 2.1e-3 step overshot, so this restores the better 1.9e-3 setting and then continues the strongest discovered trend: larger batches under a fixed-step budget. Moving from 256 to 512 batch size is a direct test of whether even more example coverage per run improves validation loss further.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.9e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The move to batch size 512 produced another large improvement, which likely increases the stable learning-rate range. A modest bump from 1.9e-3 to 2.1e-3 is the cleanest high-upside follow-up while keeping the strongest large-batch recipe intact.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.9e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2.1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.02)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.05)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The 2.1e-3 step overshot, so this restores the better 1.9e-3 setting and uses the final probe on regularization. With batch size 512 providing much stronger averaging and data coverage, slightly reducing label smoothing from 0.05 to 0.02 has a reasonable chance to improve fit and lower validation cross-entropy further.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}
